narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students—things like early grades, later academic performance, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, family income, and study plans—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 52.8% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish level, signaling to the model a dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than average at around 5.7, added to that concern as the next strongest influence in the same direction. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was close to the dataset average of 3.21 and actually pulled the prediction toward success more than any other factor, showing your college work was solid. Your LSAT score of 31.5, though under the usual 36.7, also nudged things positively, just not enough to offset the grades concerns. Being in the middle family income bracket played a minor role toward failure, while your full-time study plan helped a bit toward passing, aligning with the norm for most students. Compared to typical applicants who pass, your later grades stand out as weaker, while your GPA and LSAT are nearer the middle of the pack. This isn't a judgment on your potential—models like this highlight patterns, not your full story or resilience as a Black woman breaking barriers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later academic record through additional courses or prep, and reapplying with updated highlights of your growth; many students improve and succeed on second chances. You've got this—keep pushing.
